symbols: newborn infant — the beginning of a life destined for martyrdom, innocence preceding sacrifice
swaddling clothes — echoes the swaddling of Christ (Luke 2:7), connecting Stephen's infancy to Jesus' birth
mother reclining on bed — the ordinary miracle of birth that precedes extraordinary holiness
women attending the birth — the community of women who nurture the saints of the Church
ornate cradle — the precious care of the child who will become the first witness sealed with blood
description: A fresco detail showing a domestic birth scene in an interior room. At upper left, a woman reclines on a bed or couch, propped up and observing. In the center-right, a large female figure in a dark dress holds a swaddled infant, appearing to present or nurse the child. Below, another woman kneels beside an ornate bassinet or cradle, tending to the baby's needs. A small child or toddler stands in the lower left. The scene is rendered in muted earth tones with the warm ochre of the walls and the detailed folds of the women's garments. The composition captures a moment of quiet domestic care.
theological_description: Filippo Lippi's depiction of the birth of Saint Stephen connects the humble beginnings of the Church's first martyr to the broader narrative of Acts. Stephen, whose name means 'crown' in Greek, would become the protomartyr — the first Christian to die for his faith (Acts 7:54-60). His infancy scene is rendered with the same domestic tenderness as Nativity scenes, drawing a deliberate parallel between Stephen's birth and Christ's own. Just as Jesus was swaddled and laid in a manger, Stephen is swaddled and cradled — both beginnings that anticipate sacrificial endings. Stephen's speech before the Sanhedrin (Acts 7) recounts the entire sweep of salvation history from Abraham to Solomon, demonstrating his deep knowledge of Scripture. His dying vision of the Son of Man standing at God's right hand (Acts 7:56) and his prayer for his persecutors (Acts 7:60) mirror Christ's own death. The tradition of depicting saints' birth narratives reflects the patristic conviction that holiness is not merely achieved but providentially prepared from the beginning of life.

scripture_refs.reason: Stephen is introduced as a man full of faith and the Holy Spirit, chosen as one of the seven deacons
Stephen's martyrdom — the culmination of the life that begins in this birth scene
Stephen's vision of the Son of Man at God's right hand — the heavenly vindication of the protomartyr
Mary wrapped Jesus in swaddling clothes — the parallel between Christ's infancy and Stephen's
Before I formed you in the womb I knew you — God's providential preparation of His servants from birth
